title: Charmed Life - Chapter 9 (Fragment 1)
text:
Chapter Nine

9

B
 Y THIS TIME, Cat was as bewildered as the strange girl seemed to be. Chant? he thought. Chant? Has Gwendolen a twin sister she hasn’t told me about? “But my name’s Chant too,” he said.

“Is it, now?” said Janet. She knelt up in bed and scrubbed her hands thoughtfully about in her hair, in a way Gwendolen never would have done. “Truly Chant ? It’s not that much of a common name. And you thought I was your sister? Well, I’ve put two and two together about a hundred times since I woke up in the bath, and I keep getting five. Where are we?”

“In Chrestomanci Castle,” said Cat. “Chrestomanci had us to live here about a year after our parents died.”

“There you are!” said Janet. “My mum and dad are alive and kicking—or they were when I said good night to them last night. Who’s Chrestomanci? Could you just sketch your life history for me?”

Puzzled and uneasy, Cat described how and why he and Gwendolen had come to live in the Castle, and what Gwendolen had done then.

“You mean Gwendolen really was a witch!” Janet exclaimed.

Cat wished she had not said was. He had a growing suspicion that he would never see the real Gwendolen again. “Of course she is,” he said. “Aren’t you?”

“Great heavens, no!” said Janet. “Though I’m beginning to wonder if I mightn’t have been, if I’d lived here all my life. Witches are quite common, are they?”

“And warlocks and necromancers,” said Cat. “But wizards and magicians don’t happen so often. I think Mr. Saunders is a magician.”

“Medicine men, witch doctors, shamans, devils, enchanters?” Janet asked rapidly. “Hags, fakirs, sorcerers? Are they thick on the ground too?”

“Most of those are for savages,” Cat explained. “Hag is rude. But we have sorcerers and enchanters. Enchanters are very strong and important. I’ve never met one.”

“I see,” said Janet. She thought for a moment and then swung herself out of bed, in a sort of scramble that was more like a boy’s than a girl’s, and again quite unlike the way Gwendolen would have done it. “We’d better have a hunt around,” she said, “in case dear Gwendolen has been kind enough to leave a message.”

“Don’t call her that,” Cat said desolately. “Where do you think she is ?”

Janet looked at him and saw he was miserable. “Sorry,” she said. “I won’t again. But you do see I might be a bit cross with her, don’t you? She seems to have dumped me here and gone off somewhere. Let’s hope she has a good explanation.”

“They spanked her with a boot and took away her magic,” Cat said.

“Yes, you said,” Janet replied, pulling open drawers in the golden dressing table. “I’m terrified of Chrestomanci already. But did they really take away her magic? How did she manage to do this, if they did?”

“I don’t understand that either,” Cat said, joining in the search. By now, he would have given his little finger for a word from Gwendolen—any kind of word. He felt horribly lonely. “Why were you in the bath?” he said, wondering whether to search the bathroom.

“I don’t know. I just woke up there,” said Janet, shaking out a tangle of hair ribbons in the bottom drawer. “I felt as if I’d been dragged through a hedge backwards, and I’d no clothes on, so I was freezing.”

vocabulary.definition: confused or perplexed
vocabulary.example: Cat was as bewildered as the strange girl seemed to be.

vocabulary.definition: a large building or group of buildings fortified against attack
vocabulary.example: We live in Chrestomanci Castle.

vocabulary.definition: a habitual juxtaposition of a particular word with another word or words with a frequency greater than chance
vocabulary.example: Meaningful multi-word expressions and collocations.

vocabulary.definition: occurring frequently or ordinarily
vocabulary.example: It’s not that much of a common name.

vocabulary.definition: pulled forcibly or with difficulty
vocabulary.example: I felt as if I’d been dragged through a hedge backwards.

vocabulary.definition: left someone somewhere abruptly or unceremoniously
vocabulary.example: She seems to have dumped me here.

vocabulary.definition: people who use magic or sorcery to delight or captivate others
vocabulary.example: We have sorcerers and enchanters.

vocabulary.definition: cry out suddenly, especially in surprise, anger, or pain
vocabulary.example: Janet exclaimed that Gwendolen really was a witch.

vocabulary.definition: make (an idea or situation) clear to someone by describing it in detail or revealing relevant facts or ideas
vocabulary.example: Cat explained that hag is rude.

vocabulary.definition: the study or record of past events
vocabulary.example: Could you sketch your life history for me?

vocabulary.definition: to look carefully in different places for something
vocabulary.example: We’d better have a hunt around.

vocabulary.definition: to become involved in an activity with others
vocabulary.example: He joined in the search.

vocabulary.definition: direct one's gaze in a specified direction
vocabulary.example: Janet looked at him.

vocabulary.definition: the power of apparently influencing events by using mysterious or supernatural forces
vocabulary.example: They took away her magic.

vocabulary.definition: very unhappy or uncomfortable
vocabulary.example: He was miserable.

vocabulary.definition: informal term for mother
vocabulary.example: My mum and dad are alive and kicking.

vocabulary.definition: a spoken or written account of connected events; a story
vocabulary.example: Important narrative verbs and descriptive adjectives.

vocabulary.definition: to move hurriedly or clumsily from or into a particular place or position
vocabulary.example: She swung herself out of bed in a sort of scramble.

vocabulary.definition: try to find something by looking carefully
vocabulary.example: Cat joined in the search.

vocabulary.definition: hit with the open hand or a flat object, especially as a punishment
vocabulary.example: They spanked her with a boot.

vocabulary.definition: a feeling or thought that something is possible, likely, or true
vocabulary.example: He had a growing suspicion that he would never see the real Gwendolen again.

vocabulary.definition: very frightened
vocabulary.example: I’m terrified of Chrestomanci already.

vocabulary.definition: two offspring born of the same pregnancy
vocabulary.example: Has Gwendolen a twin sister?

vocabulary.definition: a woman thought to have magic powers, often wicked
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen really was a witch.

vocabulary.definition: men who have magical powers
vocabulary.example: Wizards and magicians don’t happen often.

vocabulary.definition: to cease sleeping
vocabulary.example: I woke up in the bath.
